shortIntroduction: I work in the gap between how a business actually operates and what its software assumes. I study the operation first, map what is really happening, design a future state the team can live with, and only then decide what should be built.
professionalNarrative: Most requirements do not arrive as requirements. They arrive as a call recording, a screenshot, three spreadsheets, a strong opinion and a deadline. My work starts there.
I enter unfamiliar industries regularly. The first job is never the software — it is understanding the business model, who is accountable for what, and which step everyone quietly works around.

I sit between the client and the people who build. I translate in both directions: business intent into implementable requirements, and technical constraint into a decision a business owner can actually make.

I stay involved past the design. Scope, estimation, delivery coordination, testing, UAT and go-live readiness are part of the same job — a process that is elegant on paper and unusable in practice has not been designed, only drawn.
I also run my own e-commerce business. Product data, stock, listings, fulfilment, returns and support are not case studies for me; they are things I have had to get wrong at my own cost.

I use AI heavily as a consulting layer — transcript analysis, requirement structuring, risk surfacing, documentation. I am not an AI engineer and do not present myself as one. The judgement is mine; the leverage is the tool's.

coreRoles.title: Business Process Consultant
coreRoles.description: Understands the business model, maps how work actually flows, finds the bottlenecks and ownership gaps, and designs a future state that people will use.

coreRoles.title: Solution Architect
coreRoles.description: Turns the agreed process into applications, modules, data relationships, automation and integration boundaries — and avoids the applications the business does not need.

coreRoles.title: Functional Consultant
coreRoles.description: Defines the behaviour: fields, statuses, blueprints, approvals, permissions, notifications, portals and reports — each with a business reason attached.

coreRoles.title: Implementation Project Lead
coreRoles.description: Carries the design into delivery: phases, estimates, dependencies, developer coordination, testing layers, UAT and go-live readiness.

roleIntersection.intro: None of these four roles describes the work on its own. The value is the overlap — and the overlap is where most projects fail.

axes.name: Business understanding
axes.description: Knowing why the process exists before deciding how to change it.

axes.name: Process design
axes.description: Structuring how work should flow, who owns each step and where the controls sit.

axes.name: System architecture
axes.description: Choosing applications, data relationships and integration boundaries that hold up under real use.

axes.name: Delivery reality
axes.description: Effort, dependencies, testing and adoption — the constraints that decide whether a design survives.

principles.title: Process before product
principles.description: The application is a consequence of the decision, not the decision itself. Choosing software early is how businesses end up with five systems and one spreadsheet that still runs everything.

principles.title: The simplest thing that holds
principles.description: Custom development is a liability with a maintenance cost attached. If standard functionality does the job at 80%, the remaining 20% needs to justify itself out loud.

principles.title: Ambiguity is the deliverable
principles.description: Anyone can document a clear requirement. The job is the unclear one — and the honest output of discovery is often a list of decisions the client has not made yet.

principles.title: Design for the person who did not ask for this
principles.description: Most users did not request the system. If the design assumes enthusiasm, it will fail. It has to work for someone doing it at 4pm on a Friday.

principles.title: Scope is a kindness
principles.description: Clear boundaries — in scope, out of scope, assumptions, dependencies, client responsibilities — protect the client as much as the delivery team. Vagueness always gets billed to someone.

principles.title: No claim without evidence
principles.description: I would rather describe what was designed and why than attach an invented percentage to it.

timeline.title: Business process and Zoho solution consulting
timeline.description: Discovery, architecture, documentation and delivery leadership across CRM and operational transformation projects, largely partner-led and international. Dates to be confirmed.

timeline.title: E-commerce and jewellery business operator
timeline.description: Running a product business end to end: catalogue, listings, inventory, marketplace channels, fulfilment, support and marketing. Dates to be confirmed.

timeline.title: AI-assisted consulting system
timeline.description: Building a reusable AI-supported method for discovery analysis, requirement structuring, documentation and delivery quality.

differentiators: The connection between process and technology
Communication that survives contact with non-technical stakeholders
Documentation that a client, a partner and a developer can all use

operatorExperience.intro: I am not only a consultant looking at commerce operations from the outside. I run an e-commerce and jewellery business, which changes how I read a requirement.
points: Product data is never as clean as the requirement assumes it is.
Stock accuracy is a process problem long before it is a system problem.
Returns and damage are not edge cases; they are a permanent share of volume.
Marketplace channels each impose their own rules on your catalogue, and they do not negotiate.
A support conversation and an order record are the same event seen from two sides.
The cost of a bad workflow is paid daily by whoever has to work around it.

aiPosition.intro: I use AI as an intelligence and productivity layer for consulting work. It is a practical position, not a specialism I claim.
doing: Turning long discovery transcripts into structured requirements and open questions
Reconciling contradictory source material into one coherent document
Generating and pressure-testing discovery questions per industry and process
Auditing documents for gaps, contradictions and unstated assumptions
Producing consistent client-facing and partner-branded documentation
Building reusable consulting frameworks and knowledge structures
notDoing: Positioning as an AI engineer or machine-learning specialist
Selling AI as the answer to a process problem
Letting a generated document leave without a human decision behind every line
